learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a thorough understanding of Australian immigration law and international refugee law.
Demonstrate a comprehensive knowledge of the sources of migration law, including relevant legislation, case law, government sources, and scholarly writing.
Understand relevant government policies in this field and in related areas.
Understand and appreciate the situation, needs, and rights of asylum seekers, refugees, and permanent and temporary migrants.
Discuss topical socio-legal issues, about immigration and refugee law/policy in an open and respectful forum.
Identify and structure relevant socio-legal issues and present them logically.
Independently research, identify, and locate relevant information and scholarly literature of the highest quality.
Present and communicate research outcomes to an audience and (where appropriate) develop informed, coherent strategies for law reform and policy change in this field of study.
Develop alternative strategies and recommendations for law reform and policy change.
Communicate clearly and efficiently in a written paper.

assessments.assessment_title: Advocacy exercise: Immigration law enforcement - Visa Cancellation Moot
assessments.due_date:
7/09/2026 - 8/09/2026

Moots will be scheduled on Monday 7th September and Tuesday 8th September, between 8am and 6pm. Moots will take place in the Moot Court on level 2 Forgan Smith.
assessments.task_description: The Moot will simulate an ART 'merits review' hearing and students will take on the role of advocates arguing about whether a visa cancellation is the preferable substantive administrative decision. Students will be provided with instructions, background facts and relevant evidence/material, along with the executive policy guidance (Ministerial Direction) and be given one week to prepare written submissions (or, pleadings) and then present the arguments (orally) in a simulated hearing room setting (Moot Court).
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will work in pairs as counsel for the Applicant or as counsel for the Respondent and will be required to submit written submissions to the Course Co-ordinator by email attachment by no later than 8am on Monday 7th Sept.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This (2500 word) summative assessment helps student's develop critical insights into the lived experiences of asylum seekers moved to offshore processing on Nauru and Manus Island in the past and encourages them to connect the lived experience (conveyed through assorted artefacts) with human rights law principles.

Students will be invited to choose from among several artefacts including 'letters from Nauru' held in the Fryer library (UQ) podcasts, auto-biographical books, cartoons or films. 

Students should plan and structure their reflective essays in the following way: (i) report (ii) relate and (iii) reason.

This involves (i) reporting on the artefact(s) chosen, conveying which artefact was chosen to inspire the reflective paper, to summarise what the artefact conveyed and why it was chosen; (ii) relating the artefact to wider socio-legal knowledge and understanding informed by prescribed reading (from earlier in the course) about offshore regional processing on Nauru and Manus Island and human rights; and (iii) reasoning, by explaining why the artefact was important in enhancing and enriching your learning – knowledge, understanding and appreciation of offshore regional processing and its human impacts.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments must be submitted electronically via the online assessment submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: The final exam will measure student understanding of the law relating to refugee status in Australia.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 13(03 Aug - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

This course will cover the following topics:

  • Week 2 - Immigration Nation: Pre-Federation Migration Controls
  • Week 3 - Immigration Nation: Post-Federation: the Commonwealth Constitutional Framework
  • Week 4 - Immigration Controls: The Constitutional Context (Executive Detention and the Separation of Powers)
  • Week 5 - Visa Requirements and General Entry Criteria
  • Week 6 - Crimmigration: The Character Test and Visa Refusals and Cancellations
  • Week 7 - The Character Test Applied (Applied Class)
  • Week 8 - Enforcing Immigration Controls: The Tampa Affair and Operation Sovereign Borders
  • Week 9 - (Workshop)
  • Week 10 - Public Holiday
  • Week 11 - Refugee Protection - Who is a Refugee under International Law?
  • Week 12 - Refugee and Complementary Protection under the Migration Act 1958
  • Week 13 - Refugee Protection under the Migration Act 1958

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09, L10
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminars This course will cover the following topics: Week 2 - Immigration Nation: Pre-Federation Migration Controls Week 3 - Immigration Nation: Post-Federation: the Commonwealth Constitutional Framework Week 4 - Immigration Controls: The Constitutional Context (Executive Detention and the Separation of Powers) Week 5 - Visa Requirements and General Entry Criteria Week 6 - Crimmigration: The Character Test and Visa Refusals and Cancellations Week 7 - The Character Test Applied (Applied Class) Week 8 - Enforcing Immigration Controls: The Tampa Affair and Operation Sovereign Borders Week 9 - (Workshop) Week 10 - Public Holiday Week 11 - Refugee Protection - Who is a Refugee under International Law? Week 12 - Refugee and Complementary Protection under the Migration Act 1958 Week 13 - Refugee Protection under the Migration Act 1958

learning_activities.topic:
Refugee Testimony from Nauru and Manus: The Lived Experience of Offshore Processing

This week our study of the lived experience of offshore processing for asylum seekers will take place in the Fryer Library at UQ at the usual seminar times, and together we will explore a unique collection of artefacts - curated letters authored by asylum seekers detained on Nauru during 2001-05 after the MV Tampa affair. We will also introduced other refugee artefacts that evidence the lived experience of forced migration and offshore processing, including podcasts, semi auto-biographical books, and artworks.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L06, L07, L10
